fact_text: Amersfort Place recalls Nieuw Amersfoort, the Dutch name for Flatlands before the British renamed it in 1664.
history_blurb: Amersfort Place takes its name from Amersfoort in the Dutch province of Utrecht. In 1636 Dutch settlers founded Nieuw Amersfoort — now Flatlands — as one of the five original Dutch towns on Long Island. The place itself is a leftover curve from the straightening of old Flatbush Road around 1890.

fact_text: Amherst Street starts Manhattan Beach's A-to-P alphabetical grid of English place names, laid out when the area was an exclusive 1870s seaside resort.
history_blurb: Amherst Street in Manhattan Beach belongs to an alphabetical series of streets with English place names running from A (Amherst) to P (Pembroke). The names were chosen when Manhattan Beach was developed as an exclusive seaside resort in the 1870s.

fact_text: Amity means 'friendship' in old English — and 197 Amity Street is where Winston Churchill's mother Jennie Jerome was born.
history_blurb: Amity Street takes its name from the English word for 'friendship.' The street was developed beginning in the 1840s with Greek Revival rowhouses. Its most notable address is 197 Amity Street — the birthplace of Jennie Jerome, who married Lord Randolph Churchill and became the mother of Winston Churchill.

fact_text: Argyle Road was plain old East 13th Street until developers renamed it — along with Rugby and Marlborough Roads — to give Flatbush a British air.
history_blurb: Argyle Road was originally East 13th Street, renamed in the early 1900s as part of the SWARM streets (Stratford, Westminster, Argyle, Rugby, Marlborough). The name comes from Argyll in western Scotland. Developers chose British place names to market the area as a refined residential enclave.

fact_text: Arion Place was renamed from Wall Street to honor Bushwick's famous German singing society — the 1886 hall is now loft apartments.
history_blurb: Arion Place was originally called Wall Street and was renamed for the Arion Mannerchor, a celebrated German men's chorus founded in 1867. The society took its name from Arion, a legendary Greek lyre player. The 1887 hall features lyre-shaped ironwork on its fire escapes and has since been converted to loft apartments.

fact_text: Arlington Avenue likely takes its name from Arlington, Virginia — the former Lee estate turned national cemetery after the Civil War.
history_blurb: Arlington Avenue runs through East New York and Cypress Hills. The name follows a common post-Civil War pattern of naming streets after Arlington, Virginia, which became nationally prominent after Robert E. Lee's estate was converted to Arlington National Cemetery in 1864.

fact_text: Ashland Place was once Raymond Street — renamed to shake the stigma of the notorious Raymond Street Jail that stood nearby.
history_blurb: Ashland Place was formerly Raymond Street, home to the infamous Raymond Street Jail that operated from the 1830s to 1963. The street was renamed to distance it from the jail's grim reputation. The name 'Ashland' likely honors Henry Clay's Kentucky estate, following a widespread 19th-century naming pattern.

fact_text: Autumn Avenue used to be Railroad Avenue, named for the nearby LIRR station before getting its seasonal rename.
history_blurb: Autumn Avenue runs from Jamaica Avenue to Liberty Avenue in East New York. It was formerly called Railroad Avenue, reflecting its proximity to the Long Island Rail Road station on Atlantic Avenue. The street was later given its current seasonal name as the neighborhood developed residentially.

fact_text: Baltic Street sits between Atlantic Ave and Pacific Street — all three named for bodies of water in Brooklyn's maritime heyday.
history_blurb: Baltic Street runs through Boerum Hill and Cobble Hill, part of a cluster of streets with maritime names including Atlantic Avenue and Pacific Street. The naming reflects Brooklyn's deep ties to shipping and waterfront commerce in the 19th century.

fact_text: Barbey Street is named for developer Andrew Barbey — his partner Sackman got a street too, about a mile to the west.
history_blurb: Barbey Street is named for Andrew Barbey of the 19th-century Barbey & Sackman real estate firm that helped build out East New York. His partner's name lives on in nearby Sackman Street. Between Sunnyside Avenue and Highland Boulevard, Barbey Street makes a dramatic S-curve up one of Brooklyn's steepest hills.

fact_text: Bath Avenue is named for Bath, England — developer B.J. Osborne wanted his 1850s seaside colony to feel just as refined.
history_blurb: Bath Avenue takes its name from Bath, Somerset, England, the town famous for its Roman-era hot springs. Developer B.J. Osborne purchased farmland along Gravesend Bay in the 1850s and established a genteel seaside resort modeled on the English spa town. The Brooklyn, Bath and Coney Island Railroad connected the resort starting in 1862.

fact_text: Battery Avenue is named for the gun batteries at Fort Hamilton — until 1898 locals could freely stroll right into the fort.
history_blurb: Battery Avenue takes its name from the artillery batteries at nearby Fort Hamilton, built in 1831 to guard the Narrows of New York Harbor. The public had open access to the fort until the Spanish-American War in 1898, when security concerns led to restricted access.

fact_text: Bayard Street honors the powerful Bayard family — Nicholas Bayard was Stuyvesant's nephew and became NYC mayor in 1685.
history_blurb: Bayard Street was formerly called Sandford Street and was renamed around 1870. It honors the prominent Bayard family of colonial New York. Nicholas Bayard (c. 1644-1707), nephew of Peter Stuyvesant, served as mayor of New York City from 1685-1686. The family became major landholders across the city.

fact_text: Beard Street honors William Beard, an Irish immigrant who built the Erie Basin and transformed Red Hook into a major shipping hub.
history_blurb: William Beard came to America in 1825 and made his fortune building railroads. Starting in 1843 he purchased waterfront land in Red Hook, filled in marshes, and built the Erie Basin — a man-made harbor that opened in 1864. Elizabeth Street was renamed Beard Street on December 14, 1865.

fact_text: The Beadels were local landowners connected to Peter Cooper, whose glue factory once dominated this part of Williamsburg.
history_blurb: Beadel Street is named for the Beadel family, who held large portions of land in the area during the mid-1850s. The family had connections to Peter Cooper, the prominent industrialist who operated a glue factory nearby — Cooper's two brothers married into the Beadel family.

fact_text: The Bedells were among Canarsie's founding families — descendants lived in the area from colonial times into the 1950s.
history_blurb: Bedell Lane is a quiet side street in Canarsie. The Bedells were one of Canarsie's earliest settler families, with Robert Bedell arriving from England in the 1600s. The family remained prominent in the area for centuries.

fact_text: Named for August Belmont, the Wall Street titan who founded the Belmont Stakes and changed his name from Schonberg to sound more French.
history_blurb: Belmont Avenue is named for August Belmont Sr., born in Germany as Aron Belmont. He began as an apprentice at the Rothschild banking house and became one of the wealthiest men in America. He chaired the Democratic National Committee for 12 years and founded the Belmont Stakes. The avenue became famous for its open-air pushcart market in Brownsville's immigrant era.

fact_text: Arthur W. Benson founded Brooklyn Gas, invested in the Brooklyn Bridge, and sold his farmland on the condition the neighborhood bear his name.
history_blurb: Benson Avenue takes its name from the Benson family whose farmland became Bensonhurst. Arthur W. Benson founded the Brooklyn Gas Light Company in 1823 and was one of only nine individual investors in the Brooklyn Bridge. He sold the land to developer James Lynch in the 1880s on the condition the neighborhood carry the Benson name.

fact_text: Named for philosopher George Berkeley — same guy Berkeley, California is named after. He pronounced it 'BARK-lee.'
history_blurb: Berkeley Place was formerly part of Sackett Street. In 1881, as Park Slope gentrified, residents petitioned for a more refined name. They chose George Berkeley, the Anglo-Irish philosopher and Bishop of Cloyne who pioneered immaterialism. The same figure inspired the naming of Berkeley, California.

fact_text: William Berriman was a newspaper man whose buddy, a local Democratic boss, got a Brooklyn street named after him.
history_blurb: Berriman Street was named for William Berriman of the Buffalo Evening News. The honor came through his friendship with Harry H. Adams, the Democratic leader of New Lots, who arranged the street naming.

fact_text: Originally just 'Avenue B,' developers gave it the posh English name Beverley in 1897 to sell more houses nearby.
history_blurb: Beverley Road was originally called Avenue B in Brooklyn's alphabetical grid south of Prospect Park. In 1897 the City of Brooklyn renamed it Beverley Road using the English spelling, after the town of Beverley in Yorkshire. Developer T.B. Ackerson was building Beverley Squares East and West nearby.

fact_text: Boerum Place honors Simon Boerum, a Brooklyn farmer turned Continental Congress delegate who helped defeat the Galloway Plan in 1774.
history_blurb: Boerum Place is named for Simon Boerum, whose family settled in Dutch New Netherland. He farmed and operated a mill in Flatbush before entering politics, serving as Kings County Clerk and delegate to the Continental Congress. He signed the Continental Association boycotting British goods.

fact_text: Boerum Street recalls Jacob Boerum, who farmed 58 acres in Williamsburg — his land was later sold to developers who built homes for German settlers.
history_blurb: Boerum Street in Williamsburg is named for Jacob Boerum, whose 58-acre farm lay in the Sixteenth Ward. The Boerum family's roots in Brooklyn trace to Willem Jacobse Van Boerum, who immigrated to New Amsterdam in 1649. Part of Jacob's farm was purchased and developed into homes for German settlers.

fact_text: Bogart Street is named for a 1650s Dutch settler — and yes, actor Humphrey Bogart was his direct descendant through seven generations.
history_blurb: Bogart Street is named for Teunis Gysbertse Bogaert, who arrived from the Netherlands in 1652 and married Sara Rapelje, said to be the first European girl born in New Netherland. He became one of Long Island's most prosperous farmers. Actor Humphrey Bogart was a direct descendant.

fact_text: Bond Street likely takes its name from Manhattan's Bond Street, itself possibly named for London's famous shopping street.
history_blurb: Bond Street in Brooklyn runs roughly a mile from Gowanus through Boerum Hill. Its naming likely echoes Manhattan's Bond Street, whose origin is debated — it may honor London's Bond Street in Mayfair or city surveyor Captain William Bond.

fact_text: Bradford Street hosted the New Lots Town Hall from 1873, which later became a police station and then a hospital for the neighborhood's poor.
history_blurb: Bradford Street runs through what was once the Town of New Lots, which seceded from Flatbush in 1852. The New Lots Town Hall was built at 109-111 Bradford Street in 1873. The building later housed a police precinct and then the Bradford Street Hospital, a walk-in clinic for the neighborhood's poor.

fact_text: Bragg Street is named for F. S. Bragg, one of the surveyors who literally drew the Sheepshead Bay street grid for Kings County in the 1870s.
history_blurb: Bragg Street honors F. S. Bragg, an engineer on the Kings County Town Survey Commission that laid out the streets of southern Brooklyn between 1869 and 1874. The cluster of named streets nearby — Bragg, Brigham, Batchelder, Coyle, Ford, Brown, Haring, and Knapp — all commemorate members of that same survey team.

fact_text: Brevoort Place recalls the Dutch Brevoort family, who took their name from the village of Bredevoort — meaning 'broad ford' — in the Netherlands.
history_blurb: Brevoort Place takes its name from the Brevoort family, whose ancestor Hendrick Janszen left the village of Bredevoort in the Netherlands around 1660 and adopted the place name as his surname. The Brevoorts became prominent Brooklyn landowners. J. Carson Brevoort maintained the family estate on Brevoort Place until 1875.

fact_text: Brigham Street is named for E. D. Brigham, a surveyor who mapped out this part of Sheepshead Bay's street grid in the 1870s.
history_blurb: Brigham Street honors E. D. Brigham, a surveyor on the Kings County Town Survey Commission that designed the street grid for southern Brooklyn between 1869 and 1874. Along with Bragg, Batchelder, Coyle, Ford, Brown, and Knapp, these streets replaced what would have been numbered avenues.

fact_text: Bristol Street was originally part of Baltic Street — renamed in 1887 when the New Lots Board of Trade reorganized 67 street names.
history_blurb: Bristol Street was created in an 1887 renaming when the New Lots Board of Trade recommended new names for 67 streets, each keeping the same initial letter as the original. Baltic Street became Bristol instead of the proposed Burton. The name likely references Bristol, England.

fact_text: Brooklyn Avenue is part of Crown Heights' Erie Canal tribute — avenues named in 1835 for cities along the canal route.
history_blurb: Brooklyn Avenue runs through Crown Heights as part of the named avenues platted in 1835 to honor cities along the Erie Canal. While neighboring avenues trace the canal from Kingston to Buffalo, Brooklyn Avenue honors the city of Brooklyn itself. By the late 1800s, wealthy merchants built mansions along the avenue near Eastern Parkway.

fact_text: Named for John Broome, a Revolutionary War militia colonel who became New York's lieutenant governor in 1804.
history_blurb: Broome Street honors John Broome, a Colonial-era merchant and politician who served as Lieutenant Governor of New York from 1804 until his death in 1810. Originally a Revolutionary War militia colonel under John Jay, Broome later became a prominent civic figure.

fact_text: Likely named for poet William Cullen Bryant, whose famous poem 'Thanatopsis' on death echoes through nearby Cypress Hills Cemetery.
history_blurb: Bryant Street is believed to honor William Cullen Bryant, the Romantic poet and longtime editor of the New York Evening Post. The street sits near Cypress Hills Cemetery, which features a path called Thanatopsis Way after Bryant's most famous poem — a meditation on death.

fact_text: Developer Dean Alvord gave this street its English name in 1899 to make his Prospect Park South subdivision feel like a country estate.
history_blurb: In 1899, developer Dean Alvord purchased about 60 acres of Flatbush farmland to create Prospect Park South with the motto 'rus in urbe' (country in the city). He renamed numbered streets with British names — Buckingham, Albemarle, Westminster, Rugby, Marlborough — to evoke an English garden suburb.

fact_text: Named in 1835 to honor the Erie Canal — seven Crown Heights avenues trace the canal route from Kingston to Buffalo.
history_blurb: Buffalo Avenue is one of seven Crown Heights avenues named in 1835 for New York State cities along the Erie Canal route. Running in geographic sequence — Kingston, Albany, Troy, Schenectady, Utica, Rochester, and Buffalo — they trace a boat's path from the Hudson River to Lake Erie.

fact_text: Named for the Dutch settlement 'Boswijck' — town in the woods — chartered by Peter Stuyvesant in 1661. It became a mansion-lined boulevard for brewers.
history_blurb: Bushwick Avenue takes its name from Bushwick, one of Brooklyn's six original Dutch towns, chartered by Governor Peter Stuyvesant in 1661 as 'Boswijck.' The land was purchased from the Canarsie Lenape in 1638. The avenue evolved into a showcase boulevard between 1880 and 1913, lined with mansions of German-American brewers.

fact_text: Part of Butler Street was renamed Sterling Place in 1873 after a clerk misspelled 'Stirling' — the old Butler name survives only in a few blocks.
history_blurb: Butler Street likely honors Benjamin Franklin Butler, the New York lawyer who served as U.S. Attorney General under Jackson and Van Buren and co-founded NYU. In 1873 the section between 5th Avenue and Flatbush was renamed Sterling Place for Lord Stirling, misspelled by a clerk.

fact_text: Butler Place kept the original name when most of Butler Street was renamed Sterling Place in 1873 — this stub survived the change.
history_blurb: Butler Place in Prospect Heights shares its origin with the longer Butler Street. When most of Butler Street was renamed Sterling Place between 1873 and 1897, this short section near Prospect Park retained the Butler name. The street sits within the Prospect Heights Historic District.

fact_text: Named for Vice President John C. Calhoun, who infamously called slavery a 'positive good' — many Calhoun streets nationwide have been renamed.
history_blurb: Calhoun Street is named for John C. Calhoun, who served as the 7th Vice President, Secretary of War, Secretary of State, and U.S. Senator. Calhoun was one of the most outspoken defenders of slavery in American politics. Many streets and institutions bearing his name have been renamed in recent years.

fact_text: Fort Greene's streets — Carlton, Adelphi, Cumberland, Portland — were all named after fashionable Westminster terraces in 19th-century London.
history_blurb: Carlton Avenue is part of a cluster of Fort Greene streets named after prestigious Westminster terraces from early 19th-century London. Carlton, Adelphi, Cumberland, Oxford, and Portland all reflect developers' aspirations during the 1850s-1860s building boom, when Fort Greene was one of Brooklyn's most prestigious neighborhoods.

fact_text: Named for the Caton family — Susan Martense Caton's mansion once stood right at Flatbush and Caton Avenues.
history_blurb: Caton Avenue honors the Caton family of old Flatbush. Susan Martense married Patrick Caton and inherited property including a house and mill at Flatbush and Caton Avenues. All three family names — Caton, Crooke, and Martense — appear on nearby Flatbush street signs.

fact_text: Caton Place is named for the same family as Caton Avenue — the prosperous Catons of old Flatbush.
history_blurb: Caton Place honors Susan Martense Caton, who lived in a mansion at Flatbush and Caton Avenues. Her father Joris Martense was an early Flatbush landholder who promoted Erasmus Academy. Caton, Crooke, and Martense streets all trace back to this prominent Dutch-colonial family.

fact_text: Like many early Brooklyn streets, Cedar Street was likely named for the cedar trees that once grew in the area.
history_blurb: Cedar Street is one of several tree-named streets in Brooklyn. Many early Brooklyn streets were named for native trees and plants found in the area during the colonial and early development periods.

fact_text: Centre Street in Brooklyn takes its name from its position near the center of the old village of Brooklyn.
history_blurb: Centre Street was named for its central location in early Brooklyn. The British spelling 'Centre' reflects the street's origins in the colonial era. It is a short street in the downtown Brooklyn area near the civic core of the old City of Brooklyn.

fact_text: Channel Avenue in Gerritsen Beach is named for the tidal waterways and channels that define the neighborhood's coastal geography.
history_blurb: Channel Avenue runs through Gerritsen Beach, a small peninsula projecting into Jamaica Bay. The street is named for the tidal channels — including Shell Bank Creek and Plumb Beach Channel — that surround the neighborhood.

fact_text: Chapel Street in downtown Brooklyn likely takes its name from an early church or chapel that once stood nearby.
history_blurb: Chapel Street is a short street in downtown Brooklyn. Its name almost certainly references a chapel or church that once stood in the area, following the common colonial-era practice of naming streets after nearby landmarks.

fact_text: Chauncey Avenue shares its namesake with Chauncey Street — Isaac Chauncey, the Brooklyn Navy Yard's longest-serving commandant.
history_blurb: Chauncey Avenue honors Commodore Isaac Chauncey (1779-1840), who commanded U.S. naval forces on Lake Ontario during the War of 1812. He served as commandant of the Brooklyn Navy Yard from 1807 to 1813 and again from 1824 to 1833.

fact_text: Cheever Place honors Samuel Cheever, one of three commissioners who laid out the street grid in Cobble Hill.
history_blurb: Cheever Place is named for Samuel Cheever (1787-1874), an attorney from Saratoga County who served as one of three commissioners responsible for laying out the grid of streets in this part of Brooklyn. The oldest houses on Cheever Place date to 1839.

fact_text: Cherry Street in Brooklyn shares its name with Manhattan's famous Cherry Street, both likely named for cherry orchards that once dotted the area.
history_blurb: Cherry Street takes its name from cherry trees or orchards that once grew in the area. In Manhattan, the parallel Cherry Street was named for a seven-acre cherry orchard planted by colonial-era landowner David Provoost.

fact_text: Chester Avenue in Brooklyn is part of a cluster of streets likely named after Chester, the historic walled city in England.
history_blurb: Chester Avenue takes its name from Chester, England. Brooklyn developers in the late 19th and early 20th centuries frequently borrowed English place names for new streets. The nearby Chester Court Historic District, designed in 1911-12, drew its name and Tudor Revival architecture from buildings in Chester, England.

fact_text: Chester Street in Brownsville is likely named after the English city of Chester, following Brooklyn's tradition of borrowing British place names.
history_blurb: Chester Street runs through the Brownsville neighborhood. Like many Brooklyn streets, it appears to be named after Chester, England. The street has been part of the neighborhood since at least the early 1900s, when Brownsville was developing as a densely populated immigrant district.

fact_text: Chestnut Avenue in Midwood is named for the chestnut trees that once grew throughout Brooklyn's landscape.
history_blurb: Chestnut Avenue is one of several tree-named streets in Brooklyn. American chestnut trees were once abundant throughout the New York region before the chestnut blight devastated the species in the early 20th century.

fact_text: Chestnut Street in East New York is named for the American chestnut trees that once flourished across Brooklyn before blight wiped them out.
history_blurb: Chestnut Street runs through East New York in Brooklyn. Like Chestnut Avenue, it is named for the American chestnut trees that were once common in the area. An earlier Chestnut Street in North Brooklyn was absorbed into DeKalb Avenue during the city's street reorganizations.

fact_text: Christopher Avenue runs through Brownsville and is likely named after a local landowner or developer from Brooklyn's early growth period.
history_blurb: Christopher Avenue is located in Brownsville, Brooklyn. While its exact namesake is not well documented, Brooklyn streets of this era were commonly named after local landowners, developers, or their family members.

fact_text: Clarendon Road was originally Avenue C, renamed with a posh British-sounding name when Flatbush developers rebranded their streets in the 1890s.
history_blurb: Clarendon Road was created when Brooklyn's lettered avenues east of Flatbush Avenue were renamed in the 1890s. Avenue C became Clarendon Road, following the pattern of giving streets elegant English names — Albemarle, Beverley, Cortelyou, Dorchester — to attract wealthier homebuyers.

fact_text: Clarkson Avenue in Flatbush was named for a local Clarkson family landowner from before the area's streets were formally laid out.
history_blurb: Clarkson Avenue in Flatbush is named for a local landowner from the era before streets were formally mapped. While Manhattan's Clarkson Street honors Revolutionary War officer Matthew Clarkson, the Brooklyn avenue appears to have a separate, more local origin tied to property ownership in the Flatbush area.

fact_text: Claver Place was renamed from Ormond Place to honor St. Peter Claver, a Jesuit priest who spent 40 years fighting the slave trade in Colombia.
history_blurb: Claver Place honors Saint Peter Claver (1580-1654), a Spanish Jesuit priest who ministered to enslaved Africans in Cartagena, Colombia for 40 years. The street was renamed from Ormond Place after St. Peter Claver Catholic Church was established there in 1921 in Bedford-Stuyvesant.

fact_text: Clay Street in Greenpoint was originally 'C Street' in an alphabetical grid, later possibly named for Senator Henry Clay or a local 17th-century pirate.
history_blurb: Clay Street runs from Front Street to Newtown Creek in Greenpoint. It was originally called 'C Street' when Neziah Bliss surveyed the streets in the 1840s using an alphabetical system. It may be named for Senator Henry Clay, who brokered the Compromise of 1850, or for Humphrey Clay, a local 17th-century pirate turned ferryman.

fact_text: Clermont Avenue is named after Robert Fulton's pioneering steamboat, the Clermont, which revolutionized travel between Brooklyn and Manhattan.
history_blurb: Clermont Avenue in Fort Greene was named for Robert Fulton's revolutionary steamboat, the Clermont, which made its famous maiden voyage on the Hudson River in 1807. The avenue was once home to the popular Clermont Rink and the Clermont Armory, longtime base of Brooklyn's elite 23rd Regiment.

fact_text: Clifford Place is a small street in Brooklyn whose exact namesake has been lost to time.
Clifton Place in Clinton Hill was likely named after the English place name Clifton, meaning 'settlement on a cliff,' following Brooklyn's British naming tradition.
history_blurb: Clifton Place is located in the Clinton Hill and Bedford-Stuyvesant neighborhoods. The name derives from the Old English 'Clifton' meaning 'settlement on a cliff.' Brooklyn developers in the mid-19th century frequently borrowed English place names.

fact_text: Clinton Avenue was named for DeWitt Clinton, the NYS governor who championed the Erie Canal — his mansion-lined street became Brooklyn's 'Gold Coast.'
history_blurb: Clinton Avenue was laid out in 1832 along the crest of Clinton Hill and named for Governor DeWitt Clinton (1769-1828), who championed the Erie Canal. Oil magnate Charles Pratt built a mansion at 252 Clinton Ave in 1874, sparking a wave of grand homes that earned the street the nickname Brooklyn's 'Gold Coast.'

fact_text: Clove Road takes its name from the Dutch word 'kloven' meaning cleft — it once followed a valley between two hills in Crown Heights.
history_blurb: Clove Road is a remnant of a colonial-era road that predates Brooklyn's street grid. Its name derives from the Dutch 'kloven' (cleft), describing the valley between two hills it once traversed. Much of the original road has been built over, but traces can still be found in Crown Heights and Flatbush.

fact_text: Clymer Street in Williamsburg is named for George Clymer of Pennsylvania, one of only six men to sign both the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution.
history_blurb: Clymer Street is part of Williamsburg's pattern of naming streets after signers of the Declaration of Independence. George Clymer (1739-1813) was a Philadelphia merchant and patriot who was one of only six men to sign both the Declaration and the U.S. Constitution.

fact_text: Coffey Street in Red Hook was renamed in 1891 for Alderman Michael J. Coffey, 'the Emperor of Red Hook,' whose political grip was so strong the area was called Coffeyville.
history_blurb: Originally called Partition Street and opened in 1848, the street was renamed in 1891 for Michael J. Coffey (1839-1907), an Irish-born alderman and state senator who dominated Red Hook politics for 39 years. His influence was so pervasive that the neighborhood was nicknamed 'Coffeyville.'

fact_text: Coleman Street in Marine Park is likely named for a local landowner or developer but its specific namesake is not well documented.
Coleridge Street in Manhattan Beach is part of an alphabetical set of English-themed street names, likely honoring poet Samuel Taylor Coleridge.
history_blurb: Coleridge Street sits in Manhattan Beach, where streets are arranged alphabetically from A to P using English place names and figures: Amherst, Beaumont, Coleridge, Dover, Exeter, and so on. The name likely honors the Romantic poet Samuel Taylor Coleridge (1772-1834), author of 'The Rime of the Ancient Mariner.'

fact_text: Coles Street in Carroll Gardens was named for Jordan Coles, a schools commissioner who owned several tide mills in the area.
history_blurb: Coles Street was named for Jordan Coles, a local schools commissioner and tide-mill owner. The street now exists in two disconnected pieces — one in Carroll Gardens and one in Red Hook — after much of it was eliminated in the 1950s by the Brooklyn-Queens Expressway.

fact_text: College Place in Brooklyn Heights was named for the Brooklyn Collegiate Institute for Young Ladies, a girls' school that stood nearby in the 1830s.
namesake: Brooklyn Collegiate Institute for Young Ladies
history_blurb: College Place takes its name from the Brooklyn Collegiate Institute for Young Ladies (1829-1842), one of Brooklyn Heights' early educational institutions. The short street leads from Love Lane and retains its quiet residential character today.

fact_text: Colonial Road in Bay Ridge was just First Avenue until early 1900s real-estate speculators gave it a fancier name to attract buyers.
history_blurb: When a subway extension to Bay Ridge was proposed in the early 1900s, it sparked wild real-estate speculation. Developers carved up farms and rebranded streets — First Avenue became 'Colonial Road' and Second Avenue became 'Ridge Boulevard' — to give the area a more upscale feel.

fact_text: Columbia Place in Brooklyn Heights takes its name from Christopher Columbus, like many 'Columbia' streets laid out in the early republic era.
history_blurb: Columbia Place was once the northern extension of Columbia Street above Atlantic Avenue. Much of it was eliminated in the 1950s when Robert Moses rerouted the BQE. Four tiny 1840s woodframe cottages at #7-13 still survive on the street today.

fact_text: Columbia Street honors Christopher Columbus — the name dates to the pre-Revolutionary era when Brooklyn Heights was considered as a seat of government.
history_blurb: The name 'Columbia' derives from Christopher Columbus and was applied during the pre-Revolutionary period when Brooklyn Heights was being considered as a potential seat of government. Columbia Street runs south from Brooklyn Heights through the Columbia Street Waterfront District, a neighborhood shaped by its Irish dockworker heritage.

fact_text: Columbus Place in Bedford-Stuyvesant is named for explorer Christopher Columbus, reflecting the 19th-century practice of honoring historical figures in Brooklyn street names.
history_blurb: Columbus Place is a short street in Bedford-Stuyvesant named for Christopher Columbus (1451-1506). Brooklyn also has a Columbus Park near Borough Hall, where a statue of Columbus was relocated in 1971.

fact_text: Commerce Street in Brooklyn likely reflects the commercial and maritime trade activity that defined the South Brooklyn waterfront in the 19th century.
Commercial Street in Greenpoint was named for the bustling waterfront commerce that defined the neighborhood, from glassworks to shipbuilding.
history_blurb: Commercial Street runs through Greenpoint's industrial waterfront, an area that once hummed with glassworks, shipbuilding, and porcelain factories. The former Chelsea Fiber Mills building on the street is now the Greenpoint Manufacturing and Design Center, preserving the area's manufacturing legacy.

fact_text: Concord Street in Downtown Brooklyn is named for the Massachusetts town where the first shots of the American Revolution were fired in April 1775.
history_blurb: Concord Street was named for the town of Concord, Massachusetts, site of one of the opening battles of the American Revolution on April 19, 1775. The Concord Baptist Church, founded by abolitionists in 1847, took its name from the street.

fact_text: Conduit Boulevard is named for the underground brick aqueduct that once carried water from Long Island to Brooklyn's Ridgewood Reservoir.
history_blurb: Conduit Boulevard was built on the former right-of-way of the Ridgewood Aqueduct, constructed in 1858 to supply Brooklyn with water. The brick conduit was 5 feet high and shaped like an inverted horseshoe. After Brooklyn joined NYC in 1898 and switched to the Croton system, the road was built over the abandoned conduit in 1929.

fact_text: Coney Island Avenue leads to Coney Island, whose name comes from the Dutch 'Konijn Eiland' meaning Rabbit Island — the Lenape called it Narrioch, 'land without shadows.'
history_blurb: Coney Island Avenue is the main road leading to Coney Island, named from the Dutch 'Konijn Eiland' (Rabbit Island) after the wild rabbits that once populated the area. The Lenape people called it Narrioch, meaning 'land without shadows,' because its south-facing orientation kept the beach in sunlight all day.

fact_text: Congress Street in Cobble Hill is believed to be named for the U.S. Congress, part of Brooklyn's tradition of patriotic street naming in the early republic.
history_blurb: Congress Street runs through Cobble Hill and Carroll Gardens. The name reflects early Brooklyn's tradition of patriotic street naming honoring American institutions. Notably, Downtown Brooklyn's Adams Street was originally called Congress Street before being renamed to honor President John Adams.

fact_text: Conklin Avenue in Canarsie was the neighborhood's original commercial center — it predates the numbered street grid and was named for an early local developer.
history_blurb: Conklin Avenue predates Canarsie's numbered street grid and once served as the neighborhood's bustling main commercial strip. As cars replaced horse-and-buggy traffic, the avenue grew too narrow for commerce and businesses migrated to Rockaway Parkway.

fact_text: Named after a Dutch colonial family, Conover Street in Red Hook traces back to 1757 landowner John Conover whose name is the same old Dutch name as Kowenhoven.
history_blurb: Conover Street was likely named after John Conover, who in 1757 owned a large property bordering a mill pond in Red Hook. The name Conover is the Anglicized form of the Dutch Kowenhoven, reflecting Brooklyn's early Dutch colonial heritage.

fact_text: Conselyea Street was cut through the Williamsburg farm of the Conselyea brothers, a family of old Dutch ancestry.
history_blurb: Conselyea Street was named for the ancestral Dutch family that owned a Williamsburg farm. In 1836 the street was extended through the Conselyea farm, and in 1855 it was extended from Bushwick Avenue to Metropolitan Avenue. The Conselyeas were part of the early Dutch settlement of the Town of Bushwick.

fact_text: Cooper Street honors industrialist Peter Cooper, who ran a glue factory nearby and later founded Cooper Union for free education.
history_blurb: Cooper Street in Brooklyn is named after Peter Cooper, a 19th-century industrialist and philanthropist who owned a glue factory in the area. In 1895 his family sold the factory site to the City of Brooklyn for $55,000, and it became Cooper Park. Cooper is best known for founding Cooper Union in 1853, a college offering free education.

fact_text: Originally named for anti-Semitic railroad baron Austin Corbin, this Brighton Beach street was rebranded as M. Corbin Place to honor Revolutionary War heroine Margaret Corbin.
history_blurb: Corbin Place was named for Austin Corbin, a railroad executive who developed Manhattan Beach into a resort that notoriously barred Jewish guests. Due to his anti-Semitism, the city added the letter 'M' to the street sign, re-dedicating it as M. Corbin Place in honor of Margaret Corbin, a Revolutionary War heroine who fought at Fort Washington in 1776.

fact_text: Cornelia Street is named for Cornelia Herring, granddaughter of Dutch settler Robert Herring, on whose farm the street was laid out in 1794.
history_blurb: Cornelia Street takes its name from Cornelia Herring, the granddaughter of Robert Herring, on whose farm the street was first laid out in 1794. The Herrings, originally Harnicks, were early Dutch settlers of New York whose land once stretched across a wide area of the city.

fact_text: Cortelyou Road honors Jacques Cortelyou, a 17th-century French Huguenot surveyor who founded the settlement that became New Utrecht in 1657.
history_blurb: Cortelyou Road is named for Jacques Cortelyou, Surveyor General of the New Amsterdam colony and a French Huguenot who founded the settlement of New Utrecht in 1657. The street was officially named in 1902; on 19th-century maps it was labeled 'Road to New Utrecht' west of Flatbush Avenue and 'Road to Canarsie' heading east.

fact_text: Coventry Road in East Flatbush is part of a tiny enclave of English-named streets created by 19th-century developers who replaced the old lettered avenues.
history_blurb: Coventry Road sits in a small enclave between Avenue B and Ditmas Avenue in East Flatbush, alongside streets like Branton and Dorset. These English-sounding names reflect 19th-century developers' practice of renaming lettered avenues with British place names to create a more distinguished neighborhood feel.

fact_text: Covert Street likely honors the Covert family, early Dutch settlers of Brooklyn whose patriarch Teunis Jans Covert emigrated from Holland in 1651.
history_blurb: Covert Street in Bushwick likely takes its name from the Covert family, early Dutch settlers of Kings County. Teunis Jans Covert emigrated from Heemstede, Holland, in 1651 and became a deacon at the Old First Dutch Reformed Church in Breuckelen.

fact_text: One of Brooklyn Heights' famous 'fruit streets,' Cranberry Street was likely named by the Hicks brothers, early landowners who sold exotic fruits.
history_blurb: Cranberry Street is one of Brooklyn Heights' beloved fruit streets alongside Pineapple and Orange. Legend says a Miss Middagh tore down aristocratic street signs and replaced them with fruit names, but the more likely story is that the Hicks brothers, local landowners and fruit sellers, named the streets after their wares in the early 1800s.

fact_text: Originally opened as Grinnell Street in 1835, this Red Hook street was renamed in 1891 for Joseph M. Creamer, a Brooklyn police surgeon.
history_blurb: Creamer Street in Red Hook officially opened in 1835 as Grinnell Street, named after Henry Grinnell, a wealthy merchant and investor in the Atlantic Dock Company. It was renamed in 1891 to honor Joseph M. Creamer, a Brooklyn police surgeon.

fact_text: Crooke Avenue honors Philip Schuyler Crooke, a Civil War brigadier general, U.S. Congressman, and longtime Flatbush resident who lived in a historic Dutch farmhouse.
history_blurb: Crooke Avenue in Flatbush is named for Philip Schuyler Crooke (1810-1881), who served 40 years in the New York National Guard rising from private to brigadier general. He led troops during the Civil War and later served as a Republican U.S. Congressman (1873-1875). He lived in the historic Schenck-Crooke House in Flatlands.

fact_text: Cropsey Avenue is named for a family of German immigrants whose descendants included Hudson River School painter Jasper Cropsey.
history_blurb: Cropsey Avenue in Bath Beach is named for the Cropsey family, one of the first to settle in New Utrecht. German immigrant Casper Crepser arrived in the 1700s and his descendants changed their name to Cropsey. The family owned Shore Parkway property for nearly 400 years, and notable descendants include the Hudson River School painter Jasper Cropsey.

fact_text: Crown Street gave its name to Crown Heights when it was extended in 1916, replacing the old neighborhood name 'Crow Hill' with something more aspirational.
history_blurb: Crown Street in Brooklyn lent its name to the Crown Heights neighborhood when the street was extended in 1916. The area was previously called Crow Hill, a name with disputed origins tied to either the crows on the hilltop or derogatory references. Developers chose 'Crown' to evoke the elevated terrain and create an upscale image.

fact_text: Cumberland Street in Fort Greene was named after Cumberland Terrace in Westminster, London, as part of a pattern of giving Brooklyn streets fashionable English names.
history_blurb: Cumberland Street is one of several Fort Greene streets named after terraces and streets in Westminster, London, including Adelphi, Carlton, Oxford, and Portland. The street became a prominent residential avenue in the 1840s-1860s as grand brownstone row houses were built to house merchants and professionals near the Navy Yard.

fact_text: Named for the cypress trees that once blanketed the hills of eastern Brooklyn — the same trees that gave Cypress Hills Cemetery its name in 1848.
history_blurb: Cypress Avenue takes its name from the abundance of cypress trees that grew on the local hills in eastern Brooklyn. The nearby Cypress Hills Cemetery, established in 1848, shares the same origin. The Cypress Avenue West Historic District includes 440 buildings from 1888-1906 and is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

fact_text: Before it was Cypress Hills this neighborhood was called Union Place after the Union Course horse-racing track that opened in 1821.
history_blurb: Cypress Hills Street takes its name from the Cypress Hills neighborhood, itself named for the tall cypress trees growing on local hills and the Cypress Hills Cemetery established in 1848. The area was originally called Union Place after the popular Union Course racetrack that opened in 1821.

fact_text: Dahill Road's Irish-origin name (from the Gaelic O Dathaill) didn't appear on Brooklyn maps until the 1920s, and historians still aren't sure exactly why.
history_blurb: Dahill Road traces an older property boundary that wound through meadows and orchards in western Flatbush. The name Dahill derives from the Gaelic O Dathaill, an Irish family from County Roscommon. Some Dahills arrived in New York in 1866, but the street did not receive its name until the 1920s.

fact_text: Dahlgren Place honors the 'father of American naval ordnance' — the city once misspelled his street sign as 'Dahlgreen' until Bay Ridge residents demanded a fix.
history_blurb: Dahlgren Place in Bay Ridge is named for Rear Admiral John A. Dahlgren (1809-1870), inventor of the Dahlgren gun and known as the father of American naval ordnance. It is one of three short streets near Fort Hamilton honoring military arms innovators, alongside Gatling Place and Parrott Place.

fact_text: Dean Street honors Silas Deane, America's first diplomat to France, who helped recruit foreign volunteers for the Revolution but died disgraced on a ship bound for home.
history_blurb: Dean Street honors Silas Deane (1737-1789), a Connecticut merchant and lawyer who became the first American diplomat sent to France. Alongside Benjamin Franklin and Arthur Lee, Deane helped secure French support for the Revolution and recruited key foreign volunteers. He died aboard a ship in Deal harbor, England in 1789.

fact_text: Debevoise Street commemorates Charles Debevoise, a village schoolmaster — the family name itself means 'Beautiful Road' in French.
history_blurb: Debevoise Street, opened in 1852 and earlier known as Banzett Street, commemorates Charles Debevoise, a village resident of Flushing Avenue and the first schoolmaster in the area. He was a descendant of Carl De Devoise, whose surname means 'Beautiful Road.' The street now runs for two blocks from Broadway east to Humboldt Street.

fact_text: Decatur Street is named for Stephen Decatur Jr., who at 25 became the youngest captain in U.S. Navy history after his daring raid in Tripoli harbor.
history_blurb: Decatur Street, named prior to 1835, honors Stephen Decatur Jr. (1779-1820), a naval officer celebrated for his heroism in the Barbary Wars and the War of 1812. Promoted to captain at age 25, he was the youngest person to reach that rank in U.S. Navy history. At least 46 communities across America bear his name.

fact_text: Degraw Street traces back to James De Graw, a Dutch-descended landowner who offered his Brooklyn farm for sale in 1764.
history_blurb: Degraw Street is named after James De Graw, an early Brooklyn landowner of Dutch descent who offered his farm for sale on April 16, 1764. The De Graw family traces back to Arent De Grauw in Amsterdam in 1574. The street name has been spelled various ways over the centuries — De Graw, Degrauw, and the current official Degraw.

fact_text: Delmonico Place may have been the old driveway to the Delmonico brothers' 220-acre Brooklyn farm, where they grew exotic vegetables like eggplant and artichoke for their famous Manhattan restaurant.
history_blurb: Delmonico Place in Williamsburg likely traces to the Delmonico brothers' 220-acre farm purchased in 1834 for $16,000. Swiss immigrants John and Peter Delmonico built an Italian villa there and cultivated vegetables unknown to Americans — endive, eggplant, artichoke — for their pioneering Manhattan restaurant.

fact_text: Dennett Place is famous for its mysterious 4-foot-tall 'hobbit doors' — and its real name might actually be Bennett Place, after a colonial family who settled Gowanus in 1636.
history_blurb: Dennett Place is a one-block alley in Carroll Gardens known for its tiny 4-foot-high 'hobbit doors' and nicknamed Cat's Alley. Built in the mid-19th century to house workers constructing the nearby church, its true name may actually be Bennett Place — the Bennetts were a colonial-era family who settled Gowanus for the Dutch in 1636.

fact_text: Dewey Place likely honors Admiral George Dewey, the only person ever to hold the rank of Admiral of the Navy, celebrated for his 1898 victory at Manila Bay.
history_blurb: Dewey Place in Stuyvesant Heights is most likely named for Admiral George Dewey (1837-1917), the only person in U.S. history to hold the rank of Admiral of the Navy. His victory at the Battle of Manila Bay in the Spanish-American War made him a national hero, and a massive two-day parade welcomed him home to New York in 1899.

fact_text: Dikeman Street, officially named in 1835, honors John Dikeman — Brooklyn's village lawyer in 1819 who went on to become a respected judge.
history_blurb: Dikeman Street, one of many streets officially named by the City of Brooklyn in 1835, was most likely named in honor of John Dikeman, one of Brooklyn's first property owners. Dikeman served as the Village's lawyer in 1819 and later became a respected judge.

fact_text: Ditmars Street in Bushwick is named for the Ditmas family, Dutch settlers who farmed Brooklyn land for over two centuries.
history_blurb: Ditmars Street takes its name from the Ditmas (originally Van Ditmarsen) family, who were among the earliest Dutch settlers in Brooklyn, arriving in Flatbush in the mid-17th century. The family farmed this land for more than two centuries and their descendants helped establish the Flatbush Plank Road.

fact_text: Division Avenue marks the old dividing line between the city of Brooklyn and the city of Williamsburg, giving it its name.
history_blurb: Division Avenue was named because it served as the dividing line between the city of Brooklyn and the town (later city) of Williamsburg. Williamsburg was founded as a separate village before becoming part of the greater city of Brooklyn.

fact_text: Dobbin Street in Greenpoint may honor Secretary of the Navy James C. Dobbin, who purchased ships built in local Brooklyn shipyards.
history_blurb: Dobbin Street in Greenpoint likely takes its name from James C. Dobbin, U.S. Secretary of the Navy (1853-1857), who purchased many ships built in local Brooklyn shipyards. An alternative theory suggests it is a corruption of 'Dobbins,' a local builder who constructed houses in the area in the 1850s.

fact_text: Dock Street in DUMBO got its name from its original function as a working dock where ships loaded and unloaded cargo.
history_blurb: Dock Street in what is now DUMBO was named for its function as a point of disembarkation for ships, dating back to the Dutch colonial period. By 1868 the Empire Stores warehouse complex was built on land between Plymouth, Main, Water, and Dock Streets, cementing the area as Brooklyn's premier waterfront commercial district.

fact_text: Dodworth Street in Bushwick likely honors the Dodworth family, famous 19th-century bandmasters who helped pioneer brass band music in America.
namesake: Dodworth family (Harvey B. and Allen Dodworth)
history_blurb: Dodworth Street in Bushwick is likely named for the Dodworth family, prominent 19th-century New York musicians. Harvey B. Dodworth (1822-1891) was a bandmaster who led the 13th Regiment Band and was the first person in the U.S. to arrange Wagner's music for military bands. His brother Allen ran the famous Dodworth's Dancing Academy on Broadway.

fact_text: Dorchester Road was originally Avenue E before being renamed in the 1890s after the English town, part of a trend to give Brooklyn streets fancier British names.
history_blurb: In the 1890s, Brooklyn's lettered avenues were renamed with British place names to give developing neighborhoods a more fashionable feel. Avenue E became Dorchester Road, joining neighbors like Cortelyou Road (Avenue C) and Ditmas Avenue (Avenue D). The renaming was driven by real estate developers seeking to attract buyers to what is now Ditmas Park.

fact_text: Dorset Street in East Flatbush is named after the English county of Dorset, following a pattern of British place names in the area.
history_blurb: Dorset Street is part of a small enclave of streets between Avenue B and Ditmas Avenue in East Flatbush that were named after British places. This followed a late 19th-century Brooklyn tradition where developers renamed lettered avenues and nearby streets with English names to attract upscale buyers.

fact_text: Doughty Street in Brooklyn Heights honors Charles Doughty, an 18th-century lawyer who helped create the Village of Brooklyn.
history_blurb: Doughty Street is named for Charles Doughty, an 18th-century lawyer who played a key role in establishing the Village of Brooklyn. The street is located in Brooklyn Heights, where many streets bear the names of early Dutch and English landholders and civic leaders.

fact_text: Despite the spelling, Douglass Street is not named after Frederick Douglass — the origin of the name predates the abolitionist's fame.
history_blurb: Douglass Street runs through Cobble Hill and Park Slope and is notably not named after abolitionist Frederick Douglass. Between 1874 and 1881, part of the street was renamed St. John's Place after St. John's Episcopal Church. In the 1920s, another orphaned section was renamed Strauss Street after Isidor Strauss, the Macy's co-owner who died on the Titanic.

fact_text: Dover Street in Manhattan Beach is part of an alphabetical series of streets named after English places, following Amherst, Beaumont, and Coleridge.
history_blurb: Dover Street sits in the Manhattan Beach section of Brooklyn, where streets were laid out in alphabetical order using English place names: Amherst, Beaumont, Coleridge, Dover, and so on. The naming convention reflects the English colonial influence on Brooklyn's development.

fact_text: Downing Street in Clinton Hill was laid out in 1799 and likely honors Sir George Downing, the same diplomat behind London's famous Downing Street.
history_blurb: Downing Street was laid out in 1799 and named no later than 1803. Though some credit it to Thomas Downing, an African American oysterman, he arrived in NYC about twenty years after the street was named. The more likely namesake is Sir George Downing (ca. 1623-1684), the diplomat who also inspired London's Downing Street and was among Harvard's first graduates in 1642.

fact_text: Drew Street in East New York sits on the old Brooklyn-Queens border, an area that was the actual city line of Brooklyn from 1886 to 1898.
history_blurb: Drew Street is located in the East New York/Cypress Hills area of Brooklyn, running along what was once the Brooklyn-Queens borough boundary line. Between 1886 and 1898, this was actually the city line of Brooklyn.

fact_text: Dumont Avenue in Brownsville may be named for Pierre Etienne Louis Dumont, a Swiss political writer and editor of Jeremy Bentham's works.
history_blurb: Dumont Avenue runs through the Brownsville and East New York neighborhoods of Brooklyn. According to Brooklyn street name records, it is named for Pierre Etienne Louis Dumont (1759-1829), a Swiss political writer and minister best known as the French editor of the philosophical works of Jeremy Bentham.

fact_text: Dunham Place honors David Dunham, the 'Father of Williamsburg' who launched the first steam ferry from Brooklyn to Manhattan.
history_blurb: Dunham Place is named for David Dunham (1790-1823), a merchant known as the 'Father of Williamsburg' for initiating the first steam ferry service from Brooklyn to Manhattan. He established the first school in Williamsburg and built a mansion near what became the street. Tragically, he drowned after falling overboard from a steamship near West Point.

fact_text: Duryea Place near the Kings Theatre in Flatbush honors the Duryea family, Huguenot settlers who arrived in Brooklyn in the 1670s.
history_blurb: Duryea Place is named after the prominent Duryea family, descended from Joost Durie, a Huguenot who emigrated from Holland and settled in New Utrecht in the 1670s. The family lived in Brooklyn for over a century and became influential community members.

fact_text: Dwight Street in Red Hook was plotted in 1835 and once had a tidal creek that divided the neighborhood into two rival camps — the 'Pointers' and the 'Creekers.'
history_blurb: Dwight Street was one of several Red Hook streets officially named and plotted by the City of Brooklyn in 1835. A tidal creek once ran along the first blocks of Dwight Street, dividing Red Hook into two communities: the 'Pointers' on the west and the 'Creekers' on the east. Fort Defiance, a Revolutionary War encampment, stood nearby.

fact_text: Dyker Place takes its name from the dikes built by 17th-century Dutch settlers to drain the marshy coastal land for farming.
history_blurb: The name 'Dyker' comes from the dikes and drainage ditches built by Dutch settlers in the 1700s to reclaim marshy coastal land. Developer Walter L. Johnson named the Dyker Heights neighborhood in 1895 after the nearby Dyker Meadows.

fact_text: Cadman Plaza honors S. Parkes Cadman, a Brooklyn pastor whose 1920s radio sermons reached millions — America's first 'radio minister.'
history_blurb: Cadman Plaza is named for Rev. S. Parkes Cadman (1864-1936), pastor of Central Congregational Church in Brooklyn. In 1923 he became the first clergyman to broadcast weekly radio sermons, reaching an audience of millions. The plaza was created in the 1950s by demolishing several blocks of buildings near Borough Hall.

fact_text: Cadman Plaza honors S. Parkes Cadman, a Brooklyn pastor whose 1920s radio sermons reached millions — America's first 'radio minister.'
history_blurb: Cadman Plaza West borders the park named for Rev. S. Parkes Cadman (1864-1936), who served as pastor of Central Congregational Church in Brooklyn. He became the first clergyman to broadcast weekly radio sermons in 1923. The plaza was built in the 1950s as part of Robert Moses' civic center plan.

fact_text: Canarsie Road follows an ancient Lenape trail — the Canarsie people farmed and fished here centuries before the Dutch arrived.
history_blurb: Canarsie Road takes its name from the Canarsie band of the Lenape, who inhabited southeastern Brooklyn. The name derives from the Lenape word meaning 'fenced land' or 'fort.' Dutch settlers purchased land from the Canarsie in the 1630s, and the area remained rural farmland well into the 20th century.

fact_text: Catherine Street honors Catherine Rutgers — her grandson Henry gave his name to a certain New Jersey university.
history_blurb: Catherine Street was named for Catherine Rutgers (1711-1779), wife of Hendrick Rutgers and matriarch of one of colonial New York's wealthiest Dutch families. Her grandson Henry Rutgers, a Revolutionary War hero, lent his name to Rutgers University. The Catherine Market, erected on the family's land in 1786, became a major public market.

fact_text: Delavan Street is a tiny Williamsburg lane — one of the shortest streets in Brooklyn, just half a block long.
history_blurb: Delavan Street is a very short street in Williamsburg, running just half a block. The origin of the name is uncertain but may reference Edward Cornelius Delavan, a prominent temperance advocate in 19th-century New York.

fact_text: Eagle Street was originally just 'E Street' in Greenpoint's alphabetical grid — Ash, Box, Clay, Dupont, Eagle, Freeman...
history_blurb: Eagle Street was originally 'E Street' in the alphabetical grid laid out by Greenpoint patriarch Neziah Bliss in the 1840s. The lettered streets were later renamed with full words: Ash, Box, Clay, Dupont, Eagle, Freeman. Eagle was named for the Brooklyn Daily Eagle, the borough's most prominent newspaper, founded in 1841.

fact_text: East New York Avenue was the main road to the village of East New York — a planned community founded by a Connecticut shoemaker in 1835.
history_blurb: East New York Avenue takes its name from the village of East New York, founded in 1835 by John R. Pitkin, a Connecticut businessman. Pitkin envisioned the area as a rival to Manhattan and named it optimistically. The avenue served as the main road connecting the new settlement to the older parts of Brooklyn.

fact_text: Eldert Street is named for the Eldert family, Dutch colonial farmers who owned large tracts in the East New York area.
history_blurb: Eldert Street and nearby Eldert Lane honor the Eldert family, early Dutch settlers who farmed extensive tracts in what is now East New York and Cypress Hills. The family name appears in colonial land records dating to the late 1600s.

fact_text: Ellery Street is named for William Ellery, a signer of the Declaration of Independence who watched each delegate sign to study their courage.
history_blurb: Ellery Street in Bedford-Stuyvesant honors William Ellery (1727-1820), a Rhode Island delegate who signed the Declaration of Independence. He famously positioned himself near the signing table to observe each signer's face for signs of fear. He later served as the first customs collector for Newport.

fact_text: Elton Street in East New York was laid out during the neighborhood's 19th-century development, likely named for a local landowner or developer.
history_blurb: Elton Street runs through East New York, part of the street grid laid out in the mid-to-late 19th century. The specific naming origin is uncertain but likely honors a local property owner or developer during the area's transition from farmland.

fact_text: Emerson Place honors Ralph Waldo Emerson, the Transcendentalist who famously urged Americans to trust themselves above all.
history_blurb: Emerson Place in Clinton Hill is named for Ralph Waldo Emerson (1803-1882), the essayist, philosopher, and leader of the Transcendentalist movement. His essay 'Self-Reliance' became one of the defining texts of American individualism. The street sits in a neighborhood dotted with literary namesakes.

fact_text: Emily Roebling Plaza honors the woman who effectively oversaw the Brooklyn Bridge's construction when her husband became bedridden.
history_blurb: Emily Roebling Plaza near the Brooklyn Bridge honors Emily Warren Roebling (1843-1903). When her husband Washington Roebling, the bridge's chief engineer, was incapacitated by caisson disease, Emily took over daily supervision. She was the first person to cross the completed bridge in 1883.

fact_text: Emmons Avenue runs along Sheepshead Bay — named for the Emmons family, who were among the area's earliest settlers and fishermen.
history_blurb: Emmons Avenue follows the shoreline of Sheepshead Bay and is named for the Emmons family, early settlers in the area. The avenue became the center of Sheepshead Bay's fishing boat industry, where party boats and seafood restaurants have lined the waterfront since the early 1900s.

fact_text: Empire Boulevard was originally Malbone Street — renamed after the worst subway disaster in NYC history killed 93 people there in 1918.
history_blurb: Empire Boulevard was originally Malbone Street, renamed after the Malbone Street Wreck on November 1, 1918, when a BRT train derailed in the tunnel beneath the street, killing 93 passengers. It remains the deadliest rapid transit disaster in U.S. history. The street was quickly renamed to erase the association.

fact_text: Erasmus Street honors Desiderius Erasmus, the Dutch Renaissance humanist — his name also graces nearby Erasmus Hall, Brooklyn's oldest school.
history_blurb: Erasmus Street in Flatbush is named for Desiderius Erasmus (1466-1536), the Dutch Renaissance humanist and theologian. Nearby Erasmus Hall Academy, founded in 1786, was one of the first secondary schools in the U.S. and counts Alexander Hamilton and Aaron Burr among its original trustees.

fact_text: Named in 2002 for Dodgers pitcher Carl Erskine — he said having a Brooklyn street named for him beat the Hall of Fame.
history_blurb: Erskine Street was co-named in 2002 for Carl Erskine (1926-2024), the beloved Brooklyn Dodgers pitcher who played from 1948 to 1959. Erskine threw two no-hitters and struck out 14 batters in a World Series game. He said he was 'beyond honored' and that having a Brooklyn street beat the Hall of Fame.

fact_text: Etna Street in East Williamsburg takes its name from Mount Etna, the famous Sicilian volcano — reflecting the area's Italian immigrant heritage.
history_blurb: Etna Street is named for Mount Etna, the active volcano on the east coast of Sicily. The naming reflects the influence of Italian immigrants who settled in the East Williamsburg and Bushwick areas in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

fact_text: Evergreen Avenue runs through Bushwick alongside Evergreen Cemetery — the green space that gave both the avenue and cemetery their names.
history_blurb: Evergreen Avenue in Bushwick takes its name from its proximity to Evergreen Cemetery, which was established in 1849. The cemetery sits on the Brooklyn-Queens border and was part of the Rural Cemetery Movement that created park-like burial grounds outside congested cities.

fact_text: Exeter Street in Manhattan Beach is part of an alphabetical A-to-P grid of English place names from the area's 1870s resort days.
history_blurb: Exeter Street belongs to the alphabetical English place-name grid in Manhattan Beach, where streets run from A (Amherst) to P (Pembroke). Named after the cathedral city of Exeter in Devon, England, the streets were laid out when Manhattan Beach was developed as an exclusive seaside resort in the 1870s.

fact_text: Fair Street in Downtown Brooklyn was part of the colonial-era street grid — its name suggests a connection to a local market or fairground.
history_blurb: Fair Street is a short street in Downtown Brooklyn. The name likely derives from a colonial-era market or fair held in the vicinity. The street was part of Brooklyn's original village grid, predating the later numbering systems.

fact_text: Falmouth Street in Manhattan Beach belongs to the alphabetical grid of English place names laid out when the area was a seaside resort.
history_blurb: Falmouth Street is part of Manhattan Beach's alphabetical English place-name series. Named after the port town of Falmouth in Cornwall, England, the streets were created when Manhattan Beach was developed as an exclusive resort destination in the 1870s.

fact_text: Farragut Road honors Admiral David Farragut, who shouted 'Damn the torpedoes — full speed ahead!' at the Battle of Mobile Bay.
history_blurb: Farragut Road in Flatbush is named for Admiral David Glasgow Farragut (1801-1870), the first rear admiral, vice admiral, and full admiral in the U.S. Navy. His famous order at the Battle of Mobile Bay in 1864 became one of the most quoted phrases in American military history.

fact_text: Fayette Street likely honors the Marquis de Lafayette, the French hero of the American Revolution, whose last name was sometimes shortened to 'Fayette.'
history_blurb: Fayette Street in Bedford-Stuyvesant is believed to be named for the Marquis de Lafayette (1757-1834), the French aristocrat who served as a general in the Continental Army. Many streets across the U.S. use the shortened 'Fayette' from his full name, Marie-Joseph Paul Yves Roch Gilbert du Motier, Marquis de La Fayette.

fact_text: Fenimore Street honors James Fenimore Cooper, author of 'The Last of the Mohicans' — nearby streets also bear literary names.
history_blurb: Fenimore Street in Prospect Lefferts Gardens is named for James Fenimore Cooper (1789-1851), the novelist who wrote 'The Last of the Mohicans' and the Leatherstocking Tales. The street is part of a Flatbush area where 19th-century developers used literary and historical names to market new homes.

fact_text: Fillmore Avenue honors President Millard Fillmore, who signed the Compromise of 1850 — a deeply controversial act that included the Fugitive Slave Law.
history_blurb: Fillmore Avenue in Marine Park is named for Millard Fillmore (1800-1874), the 13th President of the United States. He assumed office after Zachary Taylor's death in 1850 and signed the Compromise of 1850, which temporarily eased tensions between North and South but strengthened the Fugitive Slave Act.

fact_text: Fiske Place honors a local family in Park Slope — the short street features some of the neighborhood's most charming brownstones.
history_blurb: Fiske Place is a one-block street in Park Slope between Carroll Street and Garfield Place. It is named for the Fiske family, local property owners. The street is known for its well-preserved late-19th-century brownstones and lies within the Park Slope Historic District.

fact_text: Fleet Place honors Samuel Fleet, Brooklyn's postmaster and publisher — not London's Fleet Street as many assume.
history_blurb: Fleet Place is named for Samuel Fleet (1786-1864), Brooklyn Village's postmaster, librarian, and publisher of the Long Island Journal of Philosophy. Many assume the name comes from London's Fleet Street, but that's incorrect. Nearby Fleet Street follows the diagonal line of Samuel Fleet's property boundary.

fact_text: Fleet Street's diagonal path actually traces the slanted boundary of postmaster Samuel Fleet's original property line.
history_blurb: Fleet Street, like nearby Fleet Place, is named for Samuel Fleet (1786-1864), Brooklyn's early postmaster and publisher. The street's unusual diagonal orientation directly traces the slanted boundary of Fleet's property, making it one of Downtown Brooklyn's distinctive non-grid streets.

fact_text: Florence Avenue in Midwood may be named for Florence, Italy, or more likely for a developer's family member — a common naming practice.
history_blurb: Florence Avenue runs through the Midwood area of Brooklyn. The name may reference the Italian city of Florence or, more likely, a family member of one of the area's late-19th-century developers, as using women's names for streets was a common practice.

fact_text: Forbell Street honors a local landowner in the East New York area, part of the street grid laid out when the neighborhood was developed.
history_blurb: Forbell Street in East New York was named during the neighborhood's development in the mid-to-late 19th century. The name likely honors a local property owner. The area was originally part of the Town of New Lots, which separated from Flatbush in 1852.

fact_text: Force Tube Avenue is named for the pressurized water main — the 'force tube' — that runs beneath it, carrying Brooklyn's water supply.
history_blurb: Force Tube Avenue in East New York is named for the underground force tube (a pressurized water main) that runs beneath it. Like nearby Conduit Boulevard, the street was built along the route of Brooklyn's water supply infrastructure, which carried water from the Ridgewood Reservoir system.

fact_text: Fort Greene Place is named for Nathanael Greene, the Revolutionary War general Washington called his most gifted officer.
history_blurb: Fort Greene Place anchors the neighborhood named for Fort Greene, a Revolutionary War fortification named after Major General Nathanael Greene (1742-1786). Washington called Greene his most trusted general. The fort defended Brooklyn during the Battle of Long Island in 1776.

fact_text: Foster Avenue honors Charles Foster, a trustee of the United Freemen's Association who helped develop the village of Greenfield (now Parkville).
history_blurb: The original section of Foster Avenue was named for Charles Foster, a trustee of the United Freemen's Association, developers of the village of Greenfield (later Parkville). The section east of Flatbush Avenue was originally Avenue E and was renamed Foster Avenue by ordinance of the Common Council on December 10, 1901.

fact_text: Fountain Avenue in East New York was named for an actual natural spring that once flowed near its route.
history_blurb: Fountain Avenue takes its name from a natural spring or fountain that once existed along its path in East New York. The avenue later became known for the Fountain Avenue Landfill, one of New York City's largest dumps, which operated from 1961 to 1985 and has since been converted to parkland.

fact_text: Freeman Street in Greenpoint honors James Freeman, who served as a trustee of the Village of Greenpoint in the 1850s.
history_blurb: Freeman Street is named for James Freeman, one of the early trustees of the Village of Greenpoint during the 1850s. He was involved in the development of Greenpoint as it transitioned from farmland to an industrial and residential neighborhood.

fact_text: Front Street in DUMBO gets its name the old-fashioned way — it was literally the front of town, the first street back from the waterfront.
history_blurb: Front Street in DUMBO and Vinegar Hill takes its name from its position as the street fronting the East River waterfront. In colonial Brooklyn, 'Front' streets marked the edge of settlement along the water, before landfill extended the shoreline further out.

fact_text: Frost Street was formerly Bennett Street — renamed in 1912 for Edmund Frost, president of the Williamsburg Board of Trustees in the 1830s.
history_blurb: Frost Street was formerly Bennett Street, named for early Bushwick landowner William Adriana Bennett. In 1912 it was renamed for Edmund Frost, a land dealer and president of the Board of Trustees of Williamsburg in the 1830s.

fact_text: Fuller Place in Park Slope is a quiet one-block street named for a local family, lined with some of the slope's prettiest brownstones.
history_blurb: Fuller Place is a charming one-block street in Park Slope between Prospect Place and St. Marks Avenue. It is named for the Fuller family, local landowners. The street features well-preserved Victorian brownstones and sits within the Park Slope Historic District.

fact_text: Furman Street was named in 1819 for Judge William Furman — his son Gabriel wrote one of the earliest histories of Brooklyn.
history_blurb: Furman Street was named in 1819 for Judge William Furman (1765-1832), a close friend of NYS Governor DeWitt Clinton. His son Gabriel Furman (1800-1854) became a historian who wrote one of the earliest accounts of Brooklyn's history. The street runs along the East River waterfront beneath the Promenade.

fact_text: Gallatin Place honors Albert Gallatin, the longest-serving Treasury Secretary — he served under both Jefferson and Madison for 13 years.
history_blurb: Gallatin Place in Downtown Brooklyn is named for Albert Gallatin (1761-1849), who served as U.S. Secretary of the Treasury from 1801 to 1814 — the longest tenure in the position's history. The Swiss-born Gallatin also co-founded New York University.

fact_text: Gardner Avenue in East Williamsburg was named for a local property owner during the area's development in the mid-1800s.
history_blurb: Gardner Avenue runs through East Williamsburg and Bushwick. The street was laid out in the mid-19th century and likely named for a local landowner. The area was heavily industrial by the late 1800s, with factories, breweries, and warehouses.

fact_text: George S. Gelston turned a 1750 Revolutionary War headquarters into a grand 300-room hotel — and got an avenue named for him.
history_blurb: George S. Gelston came to Fort Hamilton in 1839 and bought property that included the old Hamilton House, built in 1750 and used as headquarters by both George Washington and Lord Howe during the Revolution. Gelston transformed it into a hotel with about 300 rooms.

fact_text: George Street in Williamsburg was likely named for a member of the British royal family — several King Georges ruled during the colonial naming era.
history_blurb: George Street runs through Williamsburg. Like many streets with royal first names, it was likely named during the colonial period when British monarchs' names were commonly used. Several King Georges reigned during the era when Brooklyn's streets were first laid out.

fact_text: Gerritsen Avenue honors Wolphert Gerritsen, a Dutch settler who built a tidal mill on the creek here in the 1630s — one of Brooklyn's first.
history_blurb: Gerritsen Avenue is named for Wolphert Gerritsen van Couwenhoven, a Dutch settler who arrived in New Amsterdam around 1630. He built a tide-powered gristmill on what became Gerritsen Creek — one of the earliest industrial structures in Brooklyn. The surrounding neighborhood still bears the family name.

fact_text: Gerry Street is named for Elbridge Gerry, whose creative redistricting of Massachusetts gave us the word 'gerrymandering.'
history_blurb: Gerry Street in Williamsburg honors Elbridge Gerry (1744-1814), the 5th Vice President of the United States. He is best remembered for the 1812 redistricting of Massachusetts while governor, which created a district shaped like a salamander and gave rise to the term 'gerrymandering.'

fact_text: Originally Baltic Avenue, renamed in 1887 after the hugely popular Glenmore Rod and Gun Club — every local politician was a member.
history_blurb: Originally called Baltic Avenue, the street was renamed in 1887 to eliminate duplication with Baltic Street. The name came from the Glenmore Rod and Gun Club, an immensely popular shooting association to which nearly every political leader and business owner in the Town of New Lots belonged.

fact_text: Glenwood Road was Avenue G before 1897 — renamed with an English name as Flatbush developers continued their alphabetical rebranding.
history_blurb: Glenwood Road was originally Avenue G in the lettered grid south of Prospect Park. It was renamed in 1897 as part of the same campaign that transformed Avenues A through F into Albemarle, Beverley, Clarendon, Dorchester, Ditmas (E), and Foster (F). Glenwood continued the English naming pattern.

fact_text: Gold Street was a fanciful name chosen by brothers Comfort and Joshua Sands when they bought 160 acres of Brooklyn waterfront for $12,000 in 1784.
history_blurb: Gold Street was named by Comfort and Joshua Sands, wealthy ropemakers who purchased 160 acres of Brooklyn waterfront land in 1784 for $12,000 and laid out the area's street grid. Joshua Sands went on to become a U.S. Representative and collector for the Port of New York.

fact_text: Grafton Street in East New York was part of the 1887 renaming when the New Lots Board of Trade reorganized dozens of street names.
history_blurb: Grafton Street was renamed during the 1887 New Lots Board of Trade reorganization that gave new names to 67 streets. The name may reference Grafton in England or the famous Grafton Street in Dublin, Ireland.

fact_text: Grand Army Plaza was designed by Olmsted and Vaux as the grand entrance to Prospect Park — the Soldiers' and Sailors' Arch took 3 years to build.
history_blurb: Grand Army Plaza honors the Grand Army of the Republic, the Union veterans' organization. Designed by Olmsted and Vaux in 1867 as the main entrance to Prospect Park, its centerpiece is the Soldiers' and Sailors' Memorial Arch, completed in 1892. John H. Duncan, who also designed Grant's Tomb, was the architect.

fact_text: Grand Avenue in Clinton Hill lives up to its name — it was laid out as a broad boulevard connecting the waterfront to Prospect Park.
history_blurb: Grand Avenue runs from the Navy Yard area through Clinton Hill to Prospect Park. The name reflects its intended grandeur — it was designed as a wide, tree-lined boulevard in the 19th century. The avenue passes through the Clinton Hill Historic District.

fact_text: Grattan Street is named for Henry Grattan, the Irish patriot who won legislative independence for the Irish Parliament in 1782.
history_blurb: Grattan Street in East Williamsburg honors Henry Grattan (1746-1820), the Irish politician who led the campaign for Irish legislative independence. In 1782 the British Parliament granted the Irish Parliament autonomy — the resulting 'Grattan's Parliament' lasted until the Act of Union in 1800.

fact_text: Gravesend Neck Road follows an ancient path across the 'neck' of land connecting Gravesend to Coney Island — settled by Lady Moody in 1645.
history_blurb: Gravesend Neck Road traces the colonial-era path across the narrow neck of land between Gravesend village and the ocean. Gravesend was founded in 1645 by Lady Deborah Moody, the only woman to receive a colonial land grant. She established it as a haven for religious freedom.

fact_text: Greene Avenue honors Nathanael Greene, the same Revolutionary War general who gives Fort Greene its name.
history_blurb: Greene Avenue in Bedford-Stuyvesant and Clinton Hill is named for Major General Nathanael Greene (1742-1786), George Washington's most trusted commander during the Revolutionary War. Greene's Southern Campaign is considered one of the most brilliant military operations in American history.

fact_text: Greenwood Avenue runs near historic Green-Wood Cemetery — the 478-acre landmark that's been called 'Brooklyn's Backyard' since 1838.
history_blurb: Greenwood Avenue takes its name from Green-Wood Cemetery, founded in 1838 as one of the nation's first rural cemeteries. The 478-acre grounds attracted 500,000 visitors a year by the 1860s — rivaling Niagara Falls as a tourist destination. Notable burials include Boss Tweed, Leonard Bernstein, and Jean-Michel Basquiat.

fact_text: Guernsey Street is named for Dr. Guernsey, who started a North Brooklyn newspaper in the 1850s and later became a famous homeopathic doctor.
history_blurb: Guernsey Street was named for Dr. Guernsey, who started a North Brooklyn newspaper in the 1850s and later became a renowned expert on homeopathic medicines. The street was once longer, stretching to Driggs Avenue, but was shortened around 1900 to create McCarren Park.

fact_text: Hale Avenue honors Nathan Hale, the Revolutionary War spy whose last words — 'I only regret that I have but one life to lose for my country' — became legend.
